---
title: "Plain Language Gauntlet"
subtitle: "One dense methods paragraph, three very different audiences."
description: "Take a jargon-dense pain-neuroimaging methods paragraph and make an AI translate it for a 10th grader, a patient advocate, and an NIH program officer — then ship the best one."
---

<svg viewBox="0 0 800 220" width="100%" style="max-height:220px;display:block;margin:0 0 1.2rem;border-radius:16px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#f6f8fc,#eef3fb);border:1px solid #e2e8f4">
  <defs>
    <linearGradient id="g-plain-language-gauntlet" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#0891b2"/>
      <stop offset="50%" stop-color="#e11d6b"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#7c3aed"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>
  <!-- LEFT: one dense text document = jargon-heavy source -->
  <g>
    <rect x="70" y="40" width="120" height="148" rx="8" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
    <!-- folded corner accent -->
    <path d="M170,40 L190,40 L190,60 Z" fill="#94a3b8"/>
    <!-- dense lines of text -->
    <g stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round">
      <line x1="86" y1="62" x2="174" y2="62"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="74" x2="174" y2="74"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="86" x2="166" y2="86"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="98" x2="174" y2="98"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="110" x2="158" y2="110"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="122" x2="174" y2="122"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="134" x2="170" y2="134"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="146" x2="150" y2="146"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="158" x2="174" y2="158"/>
      <line x1="86" y1="170" x2="162" y2="170"/>
    </g>
    <!-- gradient accent bar = the spark of meaning inside -->
    <rect x="86" y="50" width="60" height="5" rx="2.5" fill="url(#g-plain-language-gauntlet)"/>
  </g>
  <!-- connector lines fanning from source to three audiences -->
  <g stroke-width="2" fill="none">
    <path d="M190,90 C300,80 360,55 470,55" stroke="#0891b2"/>
    <path d="M190,114 C300,114 360,110 470,110" stroke="#e11d6b"/>
    <path d="M190,138 C300,150 360,165 470,165" stroke="#7c3aed"/>
  </g>
  <!-- connector endpoints -->
  <circle cx="190" cy="114" r="4" fill="#64748b"/>
  <!-- RIGHT, audience 1: STUDENT (speech bubble + grad cap) -->
  <g>
    <rect x="500" y="30" width="150" height="52" rx="12" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#0891b2" stroke-width="2"/>
    <path d="M512,82 L512,98 L528,82 Z" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#0891b2" stroke-width="2"/>
    <!-- grad cap icon -->
    <g transform="translate(522,56)">
      <polygon points="0,0 18,-7 36,0 18,7" fill="#475569"/>
      <path d="M30,2.5 L30,12" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/>
      <circle cx="30" cy="13" r="2" fill="#e11d6b"/>
      <path d="M8,3.5 L8,11 Q18,16 28,11 L28,3.5" fill="none" stroke="#64748b" stroke-width="2"/>
    </g>
    <line x1="572" y1="50" x2="636" y2="50" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    <line x1="572" y1="62" x2="624" y2="62" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  </g>
  <!-- audience 2: PATIENT ADVOCATE (speech bubble + person/heart) -->
  <g>
    <rect x="500" y="98" width="150" height="52" rx="12" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#e11d6b" stroke-width="2"/>
    <path d="M512,150 L512,166 L528,150 Z" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#e11d6b" stroke-width="2"/>
    <!-- person + heart icon -->
    <g transform="translate(524,114)">
      <circle cx="8" cy="6" r="6" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
      <path d="M-1,24 Q8,14 17,24" fill="none" stroke="#64748b" stroke-width="2"/>
      <path d="M30,8 a3,3 0 0,1 6,0 a3,3 0 0,1 6,0 q0,4 -6,8 q-6,-4 -6,-8 Z" fill="#e11d6b"/>
    </g>
    <line x1="572" y1="118" x2="636" y2="118" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    <line x1="572" y1="130" x2="620" y2="130" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  </g>
  <!-- audience 3: OFFICIAL (speech bubble + building/columns) -->
  <g>
    <rect x="500" y="166" width="150" height="44" rx="12" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2"/>
    <path d="M512,166 L512,154 L528,166 Z" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#7c3aed" stroke-width="2"/>
    <!-- institution / columns icon -->
    <g transform="translate(522,176)" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2" fill="none">
      <polygon points="0,6 18,-2 36,6" fill="#94a3b8" stroke="none"/>
      <line x1="2" y1="6" x2="34" y2="6"/>
      <line x1="6" y1="9" x2="6" y2="24"/>
      <line x1="18" y1="9" x2="18" y2="24"/>
      <line x1="30" y1="9" x2="30" y2="24"/>
      <line x1="-2" y1="26" x2="38" y2="26" stroke="#64748b"/>
    </g>
    <line x1="572" y1="183" x2="636" y2="183" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    <line x1="572" y1="195" x2="618" y2="195" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  </g>
  <!-- accent dots -->
  <circle cx="460" cy="55" r="3.5" fill="#0891b2"/>
  <circle cx="460" cy="110" r="3.5" fill="#e11d6b"/>
  <circle cx="460" cy="165" r="3.5" fill="#7c3aed"/>
  <!-- small crimson signature dot -->
  <circle cx="200" cy="200" r="3" fill="#b91c1c"/>
</svg>

One paragraph of dense methods-speak goes in; three clear explanations come out — and you decide which one is good enough to actually send.

<div class="meta-row">
  <span class="pill cyan">35 min</span>
  <span class="pill violet">Beginner-friendly</span>
  <span class="pill teal">teams of 3-5</span>
  <span class="pill amber">Any chat AI</span>
  <span class="pill rose">Recommended core challenge</span>
</div>

## The goal

<div class="box goal"><span class="box-label">The Goal</span>
Take the dense methods paragraph below and produce a version your <strong>assigned audience</strong> would actually understand and trust — a 10th grader, a patient advocate, or a non-specialist NIH program officer. By the end you should have <strong>one</strong> explanation polished enough that you'd be comfortable pasting it into a grant aim, a study newsletter, or a recruitment flyer. We are judging the <em>output for a real reader</em>, not the cleverness of the method you used to get there.
</div>

## Why it matters

Every translational pain scientist lives a triple life. You write Specific Aims that a program officer outside your subfield has to find compelling in 90 seconds. You sit on community advisory boards where a patient with fibromyalgia asks what your scanner actually measures. You do outreach, mentor undergrads, and explain your work at Thanksgiving. The skill of compressing "resting-state functional connectivity of the periaqueductal gray" into something a smart non-expert grasps — without misleading them — is one of the highest-leverage academic skills there is, and it is exactly the kind of task a chat AI is well suited to scaffold. Learn to drive it well here and you'll reuse the move on every abstract, lay summary, and Plain Language Statement you write.

## Run of show

<ul class="timeline">
  <li><span class="t">0:00–0:05 · Challenge introduction (5 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:05–0:20 · Work in your group (15 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:20–0:22 · Post your best prompt (2 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:22–0:32 · Share &amp; debrief (10 min)</span></li>
  <li><span class="t">0:32–0:35 · Reset (3 min)</span></li>
</ul>


## Bad prompt to better prompt

<div class="box bad"><span class="box-label">Weak prompt</span>

<div class="prompt">Explain this paragraph in simple terms: [paste methods paragraph]</div>

The AI will swap a few big words for slightly smaller ones and stop there. "Simple terms" has no audience, no length, no purpose, and no quality bar — so you get a flat, generic gloss that's still too technical for a patient and too vague for a program officer. It also keeps the paper's hedging ("we examined the association between...") instead of telling the reader why any of it matters.
</div>

<div class="box good"><span class="box-label">Strong prompt</span>

<div class="prompt">You are explaining a pain-neuroimaging study to a patient advocate on our community advisory board — a smart, motivated adult with no science background who has lived with chronic low back pain for years.

Rewrite the methods paragraph below into about 120 words at a 9th-grade reading level. Rules: no acronyms without a plain-English gloss the first time; use one everyday analogy for "functional connectivity"; lead with what we were trying to find out and why it could matter to someone in pain; never overclaim — keep it honest about what a brain scan can and can't show.

Then, in a separate section, list any sentence where you simplified something in a way a methods expert might object to, and rate your own confidence (1-5) that each sentence is still accurate.

Methods paragraph: [paste]</div>

It works because it covers the four levers the weak prompt missed: a concrete <strong>audience</strong> (with a backstory that sets vocabulary and stakes), a <strong>format</strong> (length + reading level), explicit <strong>constraints</strong> (gloss acronyms, one analogy, no overclaiming), and a built-in <strong>self-check</strong> that surfaces exactly the spots a domain expert needs to verify. You get a usable draft <em>and</em> a map of where to fact-check it.
</div>

## Prompting moves to try

- <strong>Cast the reader, not just the topic.</strong> Give the AI a one-sentence persona with stakes: "a program officer who funds basic neuroscience but isn't a pain specialist and skims 40 aims a day." Specific readers produce specific prose.
- <strong>Decompose the goal.</strong> Ask first for a glossary of the 5 hardest terms in plain English, then ask it to write the explanation using that glossary. Splitting "understand" from "rewrite" beats doing both in one shot.
- <strong>Demand an analogy budget.</strong> "Use exactly one analogy for connectivity, and flag it as an analogy" stops the AI from drowning a patient in metaphors or none at all.
- <strong>Adversarial self-evaluation.</strong> "Now critique your own version as a skeptical senior methods reviewer: what did you oversimplify, and where might a reader be misled? Score each risky sentence 1-5 for accuracy." This catches the polite-but-wrong sentences.
- <strong>Make it improve your prompt.</strong> Paste your prompt and ask: "Before answering, rewrite my instructions to get a clearer result for this audience, then proceed." You'll often learn what you forgot to specify.
- <strong>Force a contrast.</strong> "Give me two versions — one cautious, one vivid — and tell me which you'd send to this audience and why." Comparison makes the right voice obvious.

## Starter materials

<strong>The dense methods paragraph (give this identical text to every group):</strong>

<div class="box tip"><span class="box-label">Methods excerpt — "Altered descending pain modulation in chronic low back pain"</span>
Forty-two patients with chronic low back pain (cLBP) and 40 age- and sex-matched pain-free controls underwent quantitative sensory testing (QST) and resting-state functional MRI on a 3T scanner. QST included pressure pain thresholds (PPT) over the lumbar paraspinals and a conditioned pain modulation (CPM) paradigm using a cold-pressor conditioning stimulus, indexed as the change in PPT from baseline. Resting-state BOLD data (TR = 2000 ms, 6 min, eyes open) were preprocessed with motion scrubbing (FD > 0.5 mm censored), nuisance regression (24 motion parameters, white matter and CSF signals, aCompCor), and band-pass filtering (0.01–0.1 Hz). We computed seed-based functional connectivity between the periaqueductal gray (PAG), rostral ventromedial medulla (RVM), and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC), and tested group differences using a general linear model with age and framewise displacement as covariates, thresholded at a voxelwise p &lt; 0.001 with cluster-level FWE correction (p &lt; 0.05). PAG–dlPFC connectivity was negatively correlated with CPM efficiency across the full sample (r = -0.34, p = 0.002).
</div>

<strong>Audience cards (assign ONE per group):</strong>

<div class="card-grid">
  <a class="lafb-card">
    <h3>Card A · 10th grader</h3>
    <p>A curious 15-year-old in a high-school biology class. Goal: they could explain back to a friend "what these scientists did and what they found." ~100 words, no jargon, one good analogy.</p>
  </a>
  <a class="lafb-card">
    <h3>Card B · Patient advocate</h3>
    <p>An adult with chronic low back pain on your community advisory board. Goal: they trust that the study respects their experience and grasp why a brain scan is relevant to <em>their</em> pain. ~120 words, honest about limits.</p>
  </a>
  <a class="lafb-card">
    <h3>Card C · NIH program officer</h3>
    <p>A non-specialist program officer who funds neuroscience broadly. Goal: the significance and rigor land in ~90 seconds of skimming. ~120 words, keep one quantitative result, emphasize the "so what."</p>
  </a>
</div>

<strong>Quality rubric (use this to pick your one best version):</strong>

| Criterion | Failing | Solid | Excellent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Audience fit | Wrong vocabulary / level | Mostly right register | Sounds written *for* that reader |
| Accuracy | Introduces an error | Technically defensible | Honest about what's uncertain |
| Clarity | Jargon survives | Plain but flat | Plain and genuinely vivid |
| "So what" | Missing | Stated | Compelling and specific |

<strong>Self-check the AI should run before you trust it:</strong>

<div class="prompt">Re-read your explanation as the target reader. List anything they still wouldn't understand, anything that overstates what a brain scan or QST can prove, and the single weakest sentence. Then fix only those.</div>

## Debrief questions

- Which audience was hardest to write for, and why? (Most rooms find the patient advocate hardest — honesty plus warmth is a real constraint.)
- Where did the AI quietly overclaim? Did "correlated with" silently become "causes," or did a scan become a "pain detector"?
- What single line in your prompt produced the biggest jump in quality?
- Compare two groups who had the same audience but different AIs or prompts — what explains the gap, the tool or the prompt?
- Would you actually send your best version as-is? If not, what would <em>you</em> still need to fix by hand, and what does that tell you about where AI stops being trustworthy?

## Level up

- <strong>Round-trip test.</strong> Feed your plain-language version back to a fresh AI session and ask it to reconstruct the original methods. Compare to the real paragraph — what got lost or distorted is your accuracy leak.
- <strong>One source, three outputs, one prompt.</strong> Write a single reusable template with an {{AUDIENCE}} variable that produces all three versions in one go, then have the AI grade each against the rubric above.
- <strong>Ship it for real.</strong> Turn the program-officer version into the opening 3 sentences of a Significance section, and the patient version into a 50-word recruitment-flyer blurb — then critique both as if you were the IRB and the study section.

---

<p style="color:#9aa6c7"><a href="../index.qmd">← Back to the Challenge menu</a> · <a href="../intro/tools.qmd">AI Toolkit →</a></p>
